summary: In this interview, Demis Hassabis discusses the impact of AlphaFold, the future of AI in scientific discovery, and his views on classical versus quantum computing. He outlines his philosophy of treating AI as an 'engineering science' and emphasizes the importance of interdisciplinary research and the scientific method in understanding complex AI systems. In this panel discussion, Demis Hassabis shares his perspective on the future of AGI and science education. He predicts that while transformers will be a core component of AGI, additional major breakthroughs are still required. He also emphasizes the importance of exposing children to the exhilaration and fun of scientific discovery, citing Richard Feynman as a key inspiration.

principles.statement: Classical computers are capable of modeling much more complex natural systems than previously thought.
principles.rationale: Instead of naively computing every possibility, classical machine learning systems can learn the underlying structure of natural phenomena and search the possibilities efficiently, potentially even modeling quantum systems.
principles.source_quote: I believe that classical Turing machines, classical computers, are capable of a lot more than we previously thought.

principles.rationale: Unlike natural sciences where the phenomena already exist, in AI you must first build the complex artifact before you can use the scientific method to reduce and understand it.
principles.source_quote: I call it an engineering science, because unlike the natural sciences, you have to build the artifact of interest, first, and then, once you have it, you can then use the scientific method to reduce it down and understand its components.

principles.statement: Human intuition and creativity remain critical in science because AI cannot yet ask the right questions.
principles.rationale: Current AI systems are excellent tools for finding correlations and structures in data, but formulating hypotheses and asking the right questions is the hardest part of science and still requires human scientists.
principles.source_quote: I think, right now, the AI systems are just tools. I think they're great for finding correlations, and patterns, and structures in data, but for the moment, they're not able to come up with their own hypotheses or their own questions.

principles.statement: We must anchor the development and deployment of AI around the scientific method.
principles.rationale: Because AI is so powerful and transformative, we need rigorous evaluations, benchmarks, and interpretability studies rather than relying on standard tech industry A/B testing.
principles.source_quote: I think it's important we use more the scientific method than, perhaps, lean on normal sort of technology, A/B testing out in the wild.

principles.statement: Transformers are necessary but not sufficient for Artificial General Intelligence.
principles.rationale: While the transformer architecture is amazing and will be a foundational component of future AGI systems, achieving AGI will require a couple of additional, equally significant breakthroughs.
principles.source_quote: the transformer architecture... will underpin, be one of the main components of a future AGI system. But my prediction is it's not going to be enough on its own. I think we're going to need a couple of other big breakthroughs like that in addition

principles.statement: Science education must emphasize the exhilaration and fun of discovery.
principles.rationale: Exposing students to the joy of being at the frontier of knowledge, rather than just technical textbooks, is what truly inspires the next generation to pursue science.
principles.source_quote: I think it would be great for school kids to be exposed to that, how incredible and fun doing science should be

frameworks.name: AI Problem Suitability Framework
frameworks.when_to_apply: When evaluating whether a scientific or material design problem is a good fit for an AI solution.
steps: Identify a problem with a massive combinatorial search space.
Build a model that understands the underlying physics and chemistry of the natural phenomena.
Use the model to perform a highly efficient search through the combinatorial space to find an optimal solution.
frameworks.source_quote: It's massive combinatorial space. You need to try and build a model that understands the physics and the chemistry of the natural phenomena. And then, if you have a model like that, you can then maybe use it to do a very efficient search through that search space...

mental_models.name: Root Node Problem
mental_models.description: Viewing human knowledge as a tree. A 'root node problem' is a fundamental challenge that, if solved, unlocks an entirely new branch or avenue of downstream discovery.
mental_models.example: Solving the protein folding problem (AlphaFold) acted as a root node, unlocking new pathways in disease understanding, drug design, and plastic-eating enzymes.

mental_models.name: Virtual Brain Analysis
mental_models.description: Treating artificial neural networks like biological brains by applying neuroscience techniques (like an fMRI) to understand their internal workings and interpretability.
mental_models.example: Using neuroscience analysis techniques to probe the billions of parameters in a transformer model to gain mechanistic insights into how it makes decisions.

heuristics: Look for projects that do both: advance fundamental research and have a practical, positive impact on the world.
Plan many, many years ahead (the chess player mindset).

signature_quotes.text: I felt-- we sometimes call it at DeepMind, a root node problem. What we mean by that is, if you think of the whole tree of knowledge, there are certain problems, where if... you discover a solution to them, it would unlock a whole new branch or avenue of discovery.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why he chose to focus on the protein folding problem.

signature_quotes.text: I call it an engineering science, because unlike the natural sciences, you have to build the artifact of interest, first, and then, once you have it, you can then use the scientific method to reduce it down and understand its components.
signature_quotes.context: Describing how AI research differs from traditional natural sciences.

signature_quotes.text: I believe that classical Turing machines, classical computers, are capable of a lot more than we previously thought.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the potential of classical machine learning to model complex quantum systems without needing quantum computers.

signature_quotes.text: the transformer architecture... will underpin, be one of the main components of a future AGI system. But my prediction is it's not going to be enough on its own.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing whether 'attention is all you need' for the future of AI.

signature_quotes.text: I think we're going to need a couple of other big breakthroughs like that in addition, and they're still to come.
signature_quotes.context: Predicting the necessary steps to achieve AGI beyond transformers.

anti_patterns.description: A/B testing powerful AI technologies out in the wild.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: AI is too transformative and complex. It should be treated as a scientific endeavor requiring rigorous evaluation, benchmarking, and understanding of limits before deployment.

anti_patterns.description: Assuming artificial systems are easier to understand than natural systems.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Modern AI systems are just as complex as the natural phenomena scientists normally study, and breaking them down requires serious scientific effort.

anti_patterns.description: Naively brute-forcing the simulation of natural phenomena.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Trying every possibility quickly exhausts classical computation limits. Instead, you must build a model that learns the structure of the phenomena to search efficiently.

anti_patterns.description: Assuming that the transformer architecture alone is enough to achieve Artificial General Intelligence.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: AGI is too complex to be solved by a single architectural breakthrough; it will require multiple new, major breakthroughs of similar magnitude to transformers.
